title: AI Product Thinking
lesson:
AI product thinking is the skill of deciding what to build with AI and why — before you write a line of code. It is the opposite of "we have a hammer, let's find a nail." The hammer here is a large language model (a powerful text-predicting AI). The mistake is starting with the model and hunting for somewhere to wedge it. Product thinking starts with a real human problem and asks, coolly, whether AI is even the right tool.

Start with the job, not the model

Here's the first analogy. Imagine you hire a new assistant. You don't begin by listing how clever they are — you begin with the job to be done: "answer customer emails about refunds within an hour." Only then do you ask whether this person can do it, how you'll know they did it well, and what happens when they get it wrong.

AI features deserve the exact same treatment. Write the job first: who has the pain, how often, and what a good outcome looks like. If you can't write that sentence, you're not ready to build.

A blunt but useful test: would the feature still be worth shipping if it worked only 80% of the time? Many AI features are probabilistic — they guess, and sometimes guess wrong. So the best early use cases are ones where a near-miss is fine (drafting an email you'll edit) rather than ones where one wrong answer is a disaster (calculating someone's medication dose).

The probabilistic mindset

Second analogy: traditional software is a vending machine. Press B4, get the same crisps every time. AI is more like asking a knowledgeable friend — usually great, occasionally confidently wrong, never exactly the same twice. This single fact reshapes everything. A good AI product is designed around uncertainty, not in denial of it. That means three habits:

  • Keep a human in the loop where stakes are high — let the AI draft, the person approve (see Building AI Copilots).
  • Make mistakes cheap and reversible. An undo button matters more than another point of accuracy.
  • Set expectations in the UI. Calling it a "draft" or "suggestion" tells the user to check it. Calling it "the answer" sets a trap.

The build-vs-buy ladder

Beginners reach for fine-tuning (retraining a model on your own data) far too early. There's a ladder, and you climb it only when the rung below fails. Cheapest and fastest first:

Try this firstOnly if that fails
A clear prompt on an off-the-shelf API modelAdd RAG to feed in your own facts
RAG (retrieving your documents into the prompt)Tool calling so it can act, not just talk
Tools + good contextFine-tuning for a specific style or format

Why this order? Prompting changes in seconds; fine-tuning takes data, money, and weeks, and bakes mistakes in. Most shipped AI features in 2026 are not custom-trained — they are a general model plus sharp prompting, retrieval, and product design.

Measure the outcome, not the vibes

"It feels smart" is not a metric. Decide upfront how you'll know it works, in numbers a non-engineer understands. A tiny scorecard you can track from day one:

metrics = {
    "task_success_rate": 0.0,   # did the user get what they needed?
    "edit_distance": 0.0,       # how much they fixed the AI's draft
    "p95_latency_ms": 0,        # speed for the slowest 5% of requests
    "cost_per_request": 0.0,    # tokens x price (watch this scale!)
    "thumbs_down_rate": 0.0,    # quick user feedback signal
}

These tie straight to real tools: cost-per-token analysis for the budget, human evals and latency-vs-quality trade-offs for the experience.

Pick two or three numbers, watch them weekly, and let them — not opinions — decide whether to ship, tweak, or kill the feature. A feature with no metric is a feature you can't improve.

Put it together and AI product thinking is mostly old-fashioned product sense with one twist: the engine is probabilistic. Define the job, design for graceful failure, climb the cheap-to-expensive ladder slowly, and measure outcomes in plain numbers. The teams that win in 2026 aren't the ones with the fanciest model — they're the ones who picked a problem AI is genuinely good at and wrapped it in a product that makes the occasional wrong answer harmless.
takeaways: Start from the human job to be done, not the model.
AI guesses — design so wrong answers are cheap and reversible.
Climb the ladder: prompt → RAG → tools → fine-tune.

diagram.caption: Define the job, climb from cheap prompting to costly fine-tuning only as needed, then let metrics decide what to do next.

quiz.q: A teammate says: "Our app should have AI — let's find a place to put a chatbot." What does AI product thinking suggest instead?
options: Start from a real user problem and ask whether AI is even the right tool
Pick the biggest, newest model so the chatbot is impressive
Fine-tune a model first so the chatbot sounds on-brand
Add the chatbot quickly and gather feedback before defining a goal
quiz.explain: Product thinking starts with the job to be done, not the technology. 'Let's add AI' is a hammer looking for a nail; define who hurts and what good looks like first.

quiz.q: Which feature is the SAFEST first AI use case, given that LLMs are probabilistic?
options: Calculating a patient's exact medication dose automatically
Drafting a reply to a support ticket that an agent edits before sending
Auto-approving refunds with no human review
Generating final legal contract clauses sent straight to clients
quiz.explain: A draft an agent edits makes a wrong guess cheap and reversible. The other three are high-stakes cases where one confident hallucination is a disaster.

quiz.q: You need a model to answer using your company's internal policy documents. Following the build ladder, what should you try BEFORE fine-tuning?
options: Collect thousands of examples and retrain the model from scratch
Switch to a larger, more expensive model and hope it knows your policies
Use RAG to retrieve the relevant documents into the prompt
Nothing cheaper exists — fine-tuning is the only way to add private facts
quiz.explain: RAG feeds your own facts into the prompt in minutes, without retraining. Fine-tuning costs weeks and money and is a last resort, not a first move for adding knowledge.

quiz.q: Your AI feature works great in the demo but you're nervous about scaling. Which metric most directly warns you about a runaway bill?
quiz.explain: Cost per request is tokens times price, so it scales with usage and is the number that turns a cheap demo into a ruinous bill. The others track quality and speed, not spend.

mentalModel: Treat your AI feature like a new hire, not a magic box. Start from the job to be done, accept that it will sometimes be confidently wrong, and design the product so a wrong answer is cheap to catch and undo — then measure outcomes in plain numbers.
mistakes: Starting with the model, not the problem. "Let's add AI" is not a goal. Write the human job to be done first — who hurts, how often, what good looks like — and only then ask if AI is the right tool. A feature with no clear job becomes a demo nobody uses.

Pretending the output is deterministic. Traditional software gives the same answer every time; an LLM guesses and sometimes guesses wrong. If your design assumes the AI is always right, the first confident hallucination breaks user trust. Design around uncertainty instead.

Reaching for fine-tuning too early. Beginners assume custom training is the answer. Climb the ladder: prompt first, then RAG, then tools, and fine-tune last. Prompting changes in seconds; fine-tuning costs weeks and bakes mistakes in.

Shipping without a metric. "It feels smart" can't be tracked or improved. Decide upfront on two or three numbers — task success, edit distance, cost per request — and let them, not vibes, decide whether to ship.

Ignoring cost until the bill arrives. A feature that's cheap in a demo can be ruinous at scale because you pay per token. Estimate cost per request early and design prompts and retrieval to keep it low.

Choosing a high-stakes first use case. Launching AI where one wrong answer is a disaster (medical doses, legal filings) means a single error is catastrophic. Pick a forgiving first case where a near-miss is fine and a human reviews the result.
exercise:
Take any AI feature idea you have (or pick one: "summarise long Slack threads," "suggest replies to support tickets," "auto-tag expenses"). Write a one-page AI product brief with exactly five sections: (1) Job to be done — one sentence: who, how often, what good looks like. (2) Would 80% accuracy be acceptable? — yes/no and why. (3) Failure design — what happens when the AI is wrong, and how the user recovers. (4) Build ladder — could prompting alone do it, or do you truly need RAG, tools, or fine-tuning? (5) Two metrics you'd watch weekly.

Stretch goal: Estimate the rough cost per request (guess input + output tokens, multiply by a real API price) and the latency a user would tolerate. If the maths is ugly, rewrite the feature to send less text — that constraint is the design work.
solution: A strong brief is ruthless about section 2. If 80% accuracy is not acceptable, you almost always need a human-in-the-loop step (draft-and-approve) or you should pick a different feature — don't pretend the model will hit 100%. Section 3 is where beginners are weakest: a good answer names a concrete recovery path ("user edits the draft," "one-tap undo," "flag for a human"), because cheap, reversible mistakes are what make a probabilistic feature safe to ship. In section 4, the honest answer is usually "prompting plus a bit of retrieval" — if you wrote "fine-tuning," challenge yourself to explain what prompting specifically can't do, because that's rarer than people think. For the stretch goal, the lesson is that a feature dumping a whole document into the prompt every time can cost 10–100x more than one that retrieves only the relevant slice; redesigning to send less text often fixes both cost and latency at once.
summary: AI product thinking is ordinary product sense with one twist: the engine is probabilistic, so it's sometimes confidently wrong. Start from the job to be done, design so mistakes are cheap and reversible, climb the cheap-to-expensive build ladder (prompt → RAG → tools → fine-tune) slowly, and measure outcomes in plain numbers like task success, cost per request, and latency — not vibes.

keyTerms.def: Deciding what to build with AI and why — starting from a real human problem, not from the existence of a powerful model.
A one-sentence statement of who has the problem, how often, and what a good outcome looks like; the thing the feature must actually accomplish.
Behaving by guessing likely answers rather than following fixed rules, so an AI can give different — and occasionally wrong — results for the same input.
A design where a person reviews and approves the AI's output before it takes effect, used when the cost of a wrong answer is high.
The order to try solutions from cheapest to most costly: prompting, then retrieval (RAG), then tools, and fine-tuning only as a last resort.
The property that a mistake is cheap and easy to undo (an edit, an undo button), which makes a probabilistic feature safe to ship.
The share of times the user actually got what they needed from the feature — a plain-numbers outcome metric, not a vibe.
The money a single use of the feature costs, driven mainly by how many tokens (chunks of text) go in and come out; watch it as usage scales.
